name: La Mujer Muerta Circular Trail
name_original: La Mujer Muerta — circular desde Revenga
description: A difficult 19.0-mile jogging route in Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, featuring the "La Mujer Muerta" silhouette and technical terrain.
region_intro: Jogging routes in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama traverse a landscape characterized by majestic mountainous terrain, part of the Sistema Central. The region features granitic massifs, ancient glacial formations, and diverse forests of Scots pine and oak. Runners can explore high mountain grasslands, serene valleys, and areas crisscrossed by rivers like the Eresma, offering varied environments for outdoor activity. Best jogging routes in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama The most popular jogging route is Cuerda Larga Trail to La Maliciosa, a 7.9 miles (12.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 4 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers significant elevation gain and panoramic views. Another top favourite among local runners is Ribera del Río Eresma por el Puente de los Canales — circular por el Parque Nacional de la Sierra de Guadarrama, a moderate 4.8 miles (7.7 km) path. This route follows the scenic Eresma river, offering a relatively flat and accessible running experience. Local runners also love the Royal Sites Trail, a 2.8 miles (4.5 km) trail leading through historical areas, often completed in about 32 minutes. Jogging in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama is defined by granitic massifs, extensive Scots pine forests, and high mountain grasslands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy paths to challenging mountain trails. The routes in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.25 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 1000 runners have used komoot to explore Sierra Norte De Guadarrama's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama?
A: Sierra Norte De Guadarrama offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 115 trails documented on komoot. These routes cater to various preferences, from easy jogs to challenging mountain runs.

Q: Are there easy jogging routes suitable for beginners or casual runners?
A: Yes, the region provides several easy jogging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1166639295/royal-sites-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Royal Sites Trail&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 2.8-mile (4.5 km) path that takes about 32 minutes to complete, leading through historical areas. Another accessible option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1164650466/ribera-del-rio-eresma-por-el-puente-de-los-canales-circular-por-el-parque-nacional-de-la-sierra-de-guadarrama" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribera del Río Eresma por el Puente de los Canales&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 4.8-mile (7.7 km) circular route following the scenic Eresma river, known for its relatively flat terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running trails in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama?
A: The terrain is incredibly diverse, ranging from granitic massifs and ancient glacial formations to extensive Scots pine and oak forests. You'll find high mountain grasslands, serene valleys, and areas crisscrossed by rivers like the Eresma. Some routes, like those in La Pedriza, feature unique granite rock formations, while others follow historical paths such as the Roman Road of Puerto de la Fuenfría.

Q: Are there challenging trail running options for experienced runners?
A: Absolutely. Sierra Norte De Guadarrama is well-known for its challenging routes with significant elevation gains. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1234000491/cuerda-larga-trail-to-la-maliciosa" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuerda Larga Trail to La Maliciosa&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 7.9-mile (12.7 km) route with substantial elevation, offering panoramic views. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1232925969/la-mujer-muerta-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Mujer Muerta Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a long and difficult 19-mile (30.5 km) run with over 1,100 meters of ascent.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in the Sierra Norte De Guadarrama?
A: Yes, many routes in the region are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1164587462/ascent-to-la-penota-via-la-esfinge-circular-route-through-the-sierra-de-guadarrama-national-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to La Peñota via La Esfinge&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 8.8-mile (14.2 km) circular route through the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, offering stunning views.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while running in the area?
A: The region is rich in natural beauty. You can encounter glacial lagoons like Laguna Grande de Peñalara, diverse forests, and rivers. Notable highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2714437" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boca del Asno Gorge and Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt;, the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2494973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorro Grande Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, and various mountain peaks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/355629" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabeza Líjar Summit&lt;/a&gt;. The unique granite formations of La Pedriza also offer spectacular scenery.

Q: Is Sierra Norte De Guadarrama suitable for family-friendly jogging or walks?
A: Yes, the region offers trails suitable for families. Many easy paths traverse pine forests and meadows, providing a pleasant environment for all ages. The Fuenfría Valley, with its shaded pine forest trails, is particularly good for leisurely activities.

Q: What do other runners say about the jogging routes in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama?
A: The running routes in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.25 stars from over 8 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse landscapes, from challenging mountain trails with panoramic views to serene paths along rivers and through forests.

Q: Are there any waterfalls along the running trails?
A: Yes, the area is crisscrossed by rivers and features natural elements such as streams and seasonal waterfalls. You can find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2494973" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorro Grande Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1931576" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorranca Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, which can be incorporated into or viewed from various routes.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter while running?
A: The Sierra de Guadarrama National Park is an ecological treasure. Joggers might spot Iberian ibex, deer, and wild boar. The skies are home to various birds of prey, including the Spanish imperial eagle and black vulture, making for an enriching outdoor experience.

Q: When is the best time of year to go jogging in Sierra Norte De Guadarrama?
A: The region offers great running opportunities year-round, but the best time depends on your preference. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer mornings are ideal for shaded forest trails, while winter can offer unique experiences on snow-covered paths, especially at higher elevations, though some routes may require specific gear.

Q: Are there options for parking or public transport access to the trails?
A: The charming villages surrounding the natural areas, such as Cercedilla, Navacerrada, and La Granja, often serve as convenient bases for outdoor adventurers. Many trailheads are accessible by car with designated parking areas, and some villages have public transport links, making it easier to reach various starting points.
tips: The Cordal de la Mujer Muerta is formed by the peaks: Pico Pasapán (in the silhouette of the Dead Woman it corresponds to the feet), Peña del Oso (her belly) and La Pinareja (the head in the silhouette).

faqs.question: Is the La Mujer Muerta — circular desde Revenga trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this trail is rated as difficult and is best suited for experienced trail runners with very good physical fitness. It involves significant elevation changes, technical sections with rocky terrain, and sustained effort over its 30.5 km distance. It is not recommended for casual joggers or beginners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse and often rugged. You'll encounter dirt roads and forest tracks, but also technical sections with stony slopes, scree, and large boulders. Paths can be abrupt and irregular, especially along ridges. Descending parts require careful foot placement. In winter, snow and ice may necessitate specialized equipment.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the La Mujer Muerta — circular desde Revenga trail?
faqs.answer: The trail starts and ends in Revenga. While specific parking details aren't provided, you would typically look for public parking areas within or on the outskirts of the village of Revenga to begin your route.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to jog this route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for jogging this 30.5 km route is approximately 4 hours and 33 minutes (16,361 seconds). However, this can vary significantly based on individual fitness levels, weather conditions, and the technical nature of certain sections.

faqs.question: What natural features and viewpoints can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers stunning views, especially from the summits. A notable feature is the silhouette of The Dead Woman, formed by the mountains when viewed from the Segovian side. You'll also traverse beautiful pine forests like the Pinar de la Acebeda, pleasant meadows, and enjoy views of the Río Frío valley. Ridge sections provide expansive vistas but can be exposed.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: As the trail is part of the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park and passes through areas like the Pinar de la Acebeda, you might spot various wildlife. The rich ecosystem is known to host wild boars, deer, and foxes, among other species.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee for this trail in Sierra de Guadarrama National Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required for hiking or jogging on established trails within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, including this route. However, always respect park regulations, which may include restrictions on camping, fires, or group sizes. Check the official park website for the most current information before your visit.

faqs.question: Is the La Mujer Muerta — circular desde Revenga trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are often permitted on trails within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, they typically must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other trail users. Always practice Leave No Trace principles, including picking up after your pet.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog the La Mujer Muerta — circular desde Revenga trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to jog this trail is typically during spring, early summer, and autumn when the weather is milder. Summer can be very hot, especially on exposed ridge sections. Winter presents significant challenges with snow and ice, potentially requiring specialized equipment like ice axes and crampons, making it suitable only for highly experienced individuals.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several official trails. You will find sections that overlap with the Sendero Segoviano, Camino Natural de la Cañada Real Soriana Occidental, Ruta del Sureste, and the Senda del Acueducto, among others.

faqs.question: What are the winter conditions like on the trail?
faqs.answer: Winter conditions on this trail can be very challenging. Parts of the route, especially at higher elevations and on ridges, may be covered in snow and ice. This can make sections impassable without specialized equipment such as ice axes and crampons. Exposed areas are also prone to cold and strong winds.
